section.title: The Kings Defeated
section.sectionRationale: The conquest narrative closes with a roll call of victory. Chapter 12 lists the kings defeated on both sides of the Jordan: those Moses struck down in the east, and the thirty-one kings Joshua struck down in the west. It is one movement, a summary catalog that stands as a monument to what God has done, marking the transition from the taking of the land to its division among the tribes.
section.thesis: The long list of conquered kings is a testimony, not a boast: every name records a promise kept and a victory God gave, so that the whole roll, thirty-one kings on the west and two great kings on the east, stands as a memorial to the faithfulness of the God who gave His people the land.
section.frameNote: Two beats: the two kings defeated east of the Jordan under Moses, verses 1 to 6; and the thirty-one kings defeated west of the Jordan under Joshua, verses 7 to 24.

units.label: The kings defeated east of the Jordan
units.note: Sihon and Og, struck down under Moses

verses.text: Now these are the {{melek|kings}} of the land whom the Israelites struck down and whose lands they {{yarash|took}} beyond the Jordan to the east, from the Arnon Valley to Mount Hermon, including all the Arabah eastward:

Sihon king of the Amorites, who lived in Heshbon. He ruled from Aroer on the rim of the Arnon Valley, along the middle of the valley, up to the Jabbok River (the border of the Ammonites), that is, half of Gilead,
as well as the Arabah east of the Sea of Chinnereth to the Sea of the Arabah (the Salt Sea), eastward through Beth-jeshimoth, and southward below the slopes of Pisgah.
And Og king of Bashan, one of the remnant of the Rephaim, who lived in Ashtaroth and Edrei.
He ruled over Mount Hermon, Salecah, all of Bashan up to the border of the Geshurites and Maacathites, and half of Gilead to the border of Sihon king of Heshbon.
Moses, the servant of the LORD, and the Israelites had struck them down and given their land as an {{nachalah|inheritance}} to the Reubenites, the Gadites, and the half-tribe of Manasseh.

units.label: The thirty-one kings defeated west of the Jordan
units.note: so there were thirty-one kings in all

verses.text: And these are the kings of the land that Joshua and the Israelites conquered beyond the Jordan to the west, from Baal-gad in the Valley of Lebanon to Mount Halak, which rises toward Seir (according to the allotments to the tribes of Israel, Joshua gave them as an inheritance
the hill country, the foothills, the Arabah, the slopes, the wilderness, and the Negev, the lands of the Hittites, Amorites, Canaanites, Perizzites, Hivites, and Jebusites):
the king of Jericho, one; the king of Ai, which is near Bethel, one;
the king of Jerusalem, one; the king of Hebron, one;
the king of Jarmuth, one; the king of Lachish, one;
the king of Eglon, one; the king of Gezer, one;
the king of Debir, one; the king of Geder, one;
the king of Hormah, one; the king of Arad, one;
the king of Libnah, one; the king of Adullam, one;
the king of Makkedah, one; the king of Bethel, one;
the king of Tappuah, one; the king of Hepher, one;
the king of Aphek, one; the king of Lasharon, one;
the king of Madon, one; the king of Hazor, one;
the king of Shimron-meron, one; the king of Achshaph, one;
the king of Taanach, one; the king of Megiddo, one;
the king of Kedesh, one; the king of Jokneam in Carmel, one;
the king of Dor in Naphath-dor, one; the king of Goiim in Gilgal, one;
and the king of Tirzah, one. So there were thirty-one kings in all.

melek.gloss: King, ruler. Canaan was a patchwork of independent city-states, each with its own melek; the roll of thirty-one kings shows how fragmented and yet how heavily fortified the land was. Every named king is a power that stood against the LORD and fell, so the list magnifies the God who is King over all kings and gives them into His people's hand.

yarash.gloss: To take possession of, dispossess, inherit. The Israelites yarash the lands of the defeated kings, the key conquest verb that holds together gift and effort: the land is God's to give, yet it is entered and possessed as the enemy is dispossessed. The catalog records the outworking of that possession across the whole land, east and west.

nachalah.gloss: Inheritance, allotted possession. The conquered land becomes the tribes' nachalah, a heritage given by God rather than seized by right. The word bridges this summary of conquest into the coming chapters of allotment, where the inheritance is carefully divided; every king's fall clears the way for a tribe's inherited portion.

basic.context: Chapter 12 is a summary, a victory list that closes the story of the conquest before the book turns to dividing the land. It has two parts. First it recalls the two kings defeated east of the Jordan under Moses, Sihon king of Heshbon and Og king of Bashan, whose lands became the inheritance of Reuben, Gad, and half of Manasseh. Then it lists the thirty-one kings Joshua defeated west of the Jordan, city by city, from Jericho and Ai to the great northern centers, ending with the total: thirty-one kings in all. It may read like a dry catalog, but it is really a monument. Each name is a fortress that fell and a promise God kept. The question underneath is: how do we rightly remember and give thanks for what God has done?
basic.hermeneutics: This is a summary list within historical narrative, and its function is memorial and transitional. Lists like this are easy to skim, but in Scripture they often serve to record God's faithfulness in concrete detail. The chapter ties Moses' victories and Joshua's together, showing continuity in God's work, and it prepares for the allotment of the land. We read it not for battle tactics but as a testimony to a promise fully kept.
basic.originalLanguages: The chapter counts the melek, the kings, each city-state's ruler, thirty-one in the west plus two great kings in the east. Israel yarash, takes possession of, their lands. And the conquered territory becomes the tribes' nachalah, their inheritance by gift.
basic.typology: The roll of defeated kings points forward to Christ, the King of kings, before whom every earthly power will finally fall, and to the inheritance secured for God's people. The uniting of Moses' and Joshua's victories pictures the one saving work of God carried through His servants and completed in Christ.
basic.christ: Every king in this list stood against the LORD and fell; the catalog anticipates the day when every rule and authority is put under the feet of Christ, the King of kings and Lord of lords, Revelation 19:16. And the inheritance these victories secured points to the eternal inheritance won for God's people in Him.
basic.god: God is the great King who gives kings into His people's hand and fulfills His promises down to the last detail. He is faithful across generations, from Moses to Joshua. And He is the giver of the inheritance, clearing every obstacle so His people may possess what He swore to their fathers.
basic.crossReferences: Numbers 21 records the defeat of Sihon and Og under Moses. Deuteronomy 3 recalls those eastern victories. Psalm 136 gives thanks that God struck down mighty kings, for His steadfast love endures forever. And Revelation 19 names Christ King of kings, before whom every ruler falls.
basic.oneStory: The catalog of defeated kings is a monument to God's faithfulness, recording every promise kept as the land is taken east and west. It closes the conquest and opens the way to the inheritance, and it points forward to Christ, the King of kings, before whom every power will fall and in whom God's people receive their lasting inheritance.

context.literary: Chapter 12 is a hinge, closing the conquest narrative of chapters 1 to 11 and introducing the allotment section of chapters 13 to 21. It is structured as two lists: the eastern conquests under Moses, framing him as the servant of the LORD, and the western conquests under Joshua, itemized king by king to a grand total. The repeated one after each king gives the roll a drumbeat quality, a tally of comprehensive victory. The mention of the inheritance in verses 6 and 7 links the catalog forward to the division of the land.
context.historical: Canaan in this period was not a single kingdom but a mosaic of fortified city-states, each ruled by its own king, which is why thirty-one kings could be counted in a relatively small territory. Sihon and Og were formidable Amorite kings east of the Jordan whose defeat under Moses was celebrated in Israel's memory and song; Og is noted as one of the last of the Rephaim, a people of giant stature. The western list includes famous sites, Jericho, Ai, Jerusalem, Hebron, Lachish, Megiddo, Hazor, spanning the whole land from south to north. Such summary lists functioned as official records of conquest in the ancient world.
context.canonical: The chapter unites the victories of Moses and Joshua as one continuous work of God, underscoring covenant faithfulness across leaders and generations. Its theme, God striking down mighty kings for His people, is taken up in praise, notably Psalm 136, which repeats that He struck down great kings because His steadfast love endures forever. The roll of fallen kings, and the inheritance it secures, points forward to the New Testament vision of Christ as King of kings, under whom every earthly power is subdued, and to the imperishable inheritance kept for His people.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To provide a summary record of all the kings defeated in the conquest, east under Moses and west under Joshua, as a memorial to God's faithfulness in giving the land, and to mark the transition from the taking of the land to its allotment among the tribes.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes and tallies historical victories; it prescribes nothing for later readers to do. Its enduring value is as testimony: it teaches us to record and remember God's faithfulness in concrete detail, to see His work carried consistently across time and leaders, and to trust the God who keeps His promises fully.

debates.question: Why include such a detailed list of kings and cities at all?

views.label: A memorial to God's complete faithfulness
views.case: The catalog functions like the memorial stones of chapter 4: by naming every king and city, it records in concrete, unforgettable detail that God kept His promise fully, so the list is worship in the form of a ledger, each name a testimony that not one word of God's promise failed, Joshua 21:45.

views.label: An official record bridging conquest and allotment
views.case: The list serves a documentary purpose, summarizing what has been won and setting the stage for the division of the land in the following chapters; it grounds the coming allotments in the concrete reality of the territory actually conquered, so the tribes receive named, real land, not an abstraction.

debates.bearing: Both readings honor the chapter: it is at once a monument to God's faithfulness and a record that anchors the inheritance, teaching us that God's promises are fulfilled in real, nameable specifics, worth remembering and giving thanks for.

study.originalLanguages: Three words give this catalog its weight. First, it counts the melek, the kings; Canaan was not one realm but a patchwork of fortified city-states, each with its own king, so thirty-one kings in the west, added to the two great kings Sihon and Og in the east, show how densely defended the land was and how thorough the victory. Every named melek was a power arrayed against the LORD that fell before Him, so the list quietly magnifies God as the King above all kings. Second, Israel yarash, took possession of, their lands, the great conquest verb that holds together gift and effort: the land is God's to give, yet it is entered and possessed as its former rulers are dispossessed. Third, the conquered territory becomes the tribes' nachalah, their inheritance, a heritage received by God's gift rather than seized by human right; the word reaches forward into the coming chapters, where every fallen king's land is carefully allotted. Kings, possession, inheritance: the vocabulary turns a dry ledger into a testimony that the God who is King over all has given His people the land He promised.

typology.type: The roll of thirty-one kings brought down before the LORD
typology.fulfillment: Every rule and authority subdued under Christ, the King of kings and Lord of lords, 1 Corinthians 15:24-25 and Revelation 19:16.

typology.type: The inheritance secured by the fall of the kings
typology.fulfillment: The eternal, imperishable inheritance kept in heaven for the people of God in Christ, 1 Peter 1:3-4 and Ephesians 1:11-14.

typology.type: Moses' and Joshua's victories joined as one work of God
typology.fulfillment: The one saving work of God carried through His servants and completed in Christ, Hebrews 3:5-6 and 11:39-40.

study.christ: This catalog of fallen kings is a small preview of a cosmic reality. Each king in Canaan was a power that set itself against the LORD and His people, and each one fell; the roll ends with a tally, thirty-one kings in all, a monument to comprehensive victory. Scripture lifts that theme to its climax in Christ: He must reign until He has put all His enemies under His feet, and at the end He hands the kingdom to the Father, having destroyed every rule and authority and power, 1 Corinthians 15:24-25. The rider on the white horse bears on His robe and thigh the name King of kings and Lord of lords, Revelation 19:16, before whom every earthly king will bow. And the inheritance these victories secured for Israel points beyond the land to the inheritance won in Christ, imperishable and kept in heaven for His people, 1 Peter 1:4. The ledger of Joshua 12 whispers what the whole Bible proclaims: every power will fall, and the King of kings will give His people their inheritance forever.
study.god: The God revealed in this list is the great King who rules over kings and keeps His promises to the letter. Thirty-one fortified city-states and two mighty eastern kingdoms, all fell before Him, so that no power in the land could finally stand against the LORD. He is faithful across the generations, His work running unbroken from Moses in the east to Joshua in the west, one purpose carried through different servants. And He is the giver of the inheritance, clearing every obstacle so that His people may possess the land He swore to Abraham. The very dryness of the catalog is part of its testimony: God's faithfulness is not vague but concrete, nameable, countable, a promise kept city by city and king by king. This is a God whose word never returns empty, who subdues every rival, and who secures His people's inheritance in full.

crossReferences.note: The defeat of Sihon and Og under Moses, the two eastern kings this chapter recalls.
Moses recounts the victory over Og of Bashan, one of the last of the Rephaim, echoed here.
Give thanks to God who struck down mighty kings and gave their land as a heritage, for His steadfast love endures forever.
Not one word of all the good promises of the LORD failed; the catalog is that faithfulness in concrete detail.
Christ is named King of kings and Lord of lords, before whom every ruler falls, the fulfillment the list foreshadows.

study.oneStory: The roll of defeated kings is a monument to God's faithfulness, tallying every fortress that fell and every promise kept as the land is taken east and west. It closes the conquest and opens the way to the inheritance the tribes will receive. And it points beyond itself to Christ, the King of kings, under whom every power will finally fall, and in whom the people of God receive an inheritance that can never be lost.

options.text: A summary list of all the kings Israel defeated, east under Moses and west under Joshua.
options.note: The chapter catalogs the conquered kings on both sides of the Jordan as a memorial of victory.

options.text: A detailed account of a single new battle.
options.note: It summarizes many past victories rather than narrating one new battle.

options.text: A list of the tribes and their leaders.
options.note: It lists defeated kings, not the tribes and their leaders.
It is a catalog of defeated kings, not a collection of prayers.

quiz.q: Which two great kings east of the Jordan does the chapter recall, and under whom did they fall?

options.text: Sihon and Og, defeated under Moses, whose land went to the eastern tribes.
options.note: Verses 2 to 6 recall Sihon and Og struck down under Moses.

options.text: Adoni-zedek and Jabin, defeated under Joshua.
options.note: Those kings fell under Joshua in the west; the eastern kings recalled here are Sihon and Og under Moses.

options.text: The kings of Jericho and Ai, defeated under Moses.
options.note: Jericho and Ai fell under Joshua in the west; the eastern kings were Sihon and Og under Moses.

options.text: No kings are mentioned east of the Jordan.
options.note: Sihon and Og are specifically named as the eastern kings defeated under Moses.

quiz.q: How many kings did Joshua defeat west of the Jordan?

options.note: Verse 24 gives the total, thirty-one kings, a tally of comprehensive victory.

options.text: Exactly seven kings, one for each nation.
options.note: There were seven nations, but thirty-one individual city-kings were defeated.
Sihon and Og fell in the east under Moses; the western total under Joshua was thirty-one.
The stated total is thirty-one kings, not over a hundred.

quiz.q: Why does Scripture include such a detailed catalog of kings and cities?

options.text: As a memorial to God's complete faithfulness, recording that not one of His promises failed.
options.note: The list is a concrete testimony that God kept His promise to give the land, city by city.

options.text: To brag about Israel's military genius.
options.note: The list testifies to God's faithfulness, not Israel's genius; the victories are credited to the LORD.

options.text: To provide directions for future travelers.
options.note: It is a memorial of conquest, not a travel guide.

options.text: For no particular reason; it is filler.
options.note: The catalog is a deliberate memorial of God's faithfulness and a bridge to the allotment of the land.

questions: The list turns victory into a written memorial. What specific answers to prayer or acts of God's faithfulness could you write down and keep as your own record of what He has done?
Every named king was a power that fell before the LORD. What seemingly immovable obstacle in your life do you need to trust to the God who brings down kings?
The chapter joins Moses' victories to Joshua's as one work of God. How does it encourage you to see God working consistently across different seasons and leaders in your own life and church?
The fallen kings point to Christ, the King of kings, under whom every power will finally bow. How does that future certainty shape the way you face opposition now?
The conquered land became the tribes' inheritance. How does the promise of an imperishable inheritance in Christ steady you when earthly things feel uncertain?

translationNotes.note: The Sea of Chinnereth is the Sea of Galilee, and the Sea of the Arabah, the Salt Sea, is the Dead Sea, as the BSB footnotes note.

The BSB footnote notes that the Septuagint reads Og king of Bashan, while the Hebrew reads the territory of Og king of Bashan; Og is noted as one of the last of the Rephaim, a people of great stature.

The BSB sets off the lands of the peoples with an em dash; this has been rendered as a comma here, with no change to the wording. The verses of the king-list, joined from short poetic lines, have been rendered as running text separated by semicolons.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Joshua 12:1-24. Summary list; the two-column king-list in verses 9 to 24 was joined in order as running text with the line-break markers rendered as semicolons. The em dash in verse 8 was converted to a comma; no em or en dashes remain anywhere in the file. Sermon left as none; no sermon URL was verified this session.
